raw_arguments.content:
###############################################################################
# Adaptive Rejection Sampling (ARS)
# Based on Gilks, W.R. & Wild, P. (1992)
# "Adaptive Rejection Sampling for Gibbs Sampling"
# Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series C, 41(2), 337-348.
###############################################################################

# --- Auxiliary / Module Functions ---

#' Evaluate the log of a density function safely
#' @param f density function (possibly unnormalized)
#' @param x point(s) at which to evaluate
#' @return log(f(x)), with protection against f(x) <= 0
eval_log_density <- function(f, x) {
  fx <- f(x)
  if (any(is.na(fx) | is.nan(fx))) {
    stop("Density function returned NA/NaN.")
  }
  if (any(fx < 0)) {
    stop("Density function returned negative value.")
  }
  result <- rep(-Inf, length(fx))
  pos <- fx > 0
  result[pos] <- log(fx[pos])
  return(result)
}

#' Check log-concavity at three consecutive abscissae
#' If h(x) = log(f(x)), then log-concavity requires:
#'   (h(x2) - h(x1))/(x2 - x1) >= (h(x3) - h(x2))/(x3 - x2)
#' for all x1 < x2 < x3
#' @param abscissae sorted vector of x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding log-density values h(x)
#' @return TRUE if log-concave, else stops with error
check_log_concavity <- function(abscissae, h_vals) {
  n <- length(abscissae)
  if (n < 3) return(TRUE)
  for (i in 1:(n - 2)) {
    x1 <- abscissae[i];   h1 <- h_vals[i]
    x2 <- abscissae[i+1]; h2 <- h_vals[i+1]
    x3 <- abscissae[i+2]; h3 <- h_vals[i+2]
    slope_left  <- (h2 - h1) / (x2 - x1)
    slope_right <- (h3 - h2) / (x3 - x2)
    if (slope_right > slope_left + 1e-10) {
      stop(paste0(
        "Log-concavity violated at x = ", x2,
        ". Left slope = ", round(slope_left, 6),
        ", Right slope = ", round(slope_right, 6),
        ". The density may not be log-concave."
      ))
    }
  }
  return(TRUE)
}

#' Compute the slopes between consecutive abscissae on the log-density
#' @param abscissae sorted x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding h(x)
#' @return vector of slopes of length (n-1)
compute_slopes <- function(abscissae, h_vals) {
  n <- length(abscissae)
  slopes <- (h_vals[2:n] - h_vals[1:(n-1)]) / (abscissae[2:n] - abscissae[1:(n-1)])
  return(slopes)
}

#' Compute the upper hull (piecewise-linear envelope)
#' The upper hull at each abscissa j uses the tangent lines from adjacent segments.
#' For j=1: use slope from segment (x1, x2)
#' For j=k (last): use slope from segment (x_{k-1}, x_k)
#' For interior j: the tangent from left segment and right segment
#'
#' Returns the intersection points z between consecutive tangent lines,
#' plus the slopes and intercepts of each tangent segment.
#'
#' @param abscissae sorted x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding h(x)
#' @param lb lower bound of domain
#' @param ub upper bound of domain
#' @return list with z (intersection points), slopes, intercepts for upper hull
compute_upper_hull <- function(abscissae, h_vals, lb, ub) {
  k <- length(abscissae)

  # Slopes of secant lines between consecutive abscissae
  secant_slopes <- compute_slopes(abscissae, h_vals)

  # Each tangent line j: u_j(x) = h_j + s_j * (x - x_j)
  # where s_j is the slope assigned to abscissa j
  # For the upper hull in ARS:
  #   - segment j uses the line through (x_j, h_j) and (x_{j+1}, h_{j+1})
  #   i.e., slope = secant_slope[j], intercept at x_j
  # There are k-1 segments. The hull extends to lb on the left and ub on the right.

  # The upper hull consists of k-1 line segments connecting consecutive points.
  # But in ARS, the upper hull is defined by tangent lines at each abscissa.
  # For log-concave densities, the tangent at x_j has slope between
  # the left secant and right secant.

  # Actually, in Gilks & Wild (1992), the upper hull u_k(x) is the
  # minimum of the tangent lines. With only function values (no derivatives),
  # they use the secant lines. Specifically:
  #
  # The upper hull is piecewise linear with pieces defined by:
  #   piece j (for j = 1, ..., k-1): line through (x_j, h_j) and (x_{j+1}, h_{j+1})
  #
  # The intersection z_j of piece j and piece j+1 gives the breakpoints.

  # For j = 1,...,k-1, line j has:
  #   slope_j = (h_{j+1} - h_j) / (x_{j+1} - x_j)
  #   u_j(x) = h_j + slope_j * (x - x_j)
  #
  # Intersection of line j and line j+1:
  #   h_j + slope_j * (z - x_j) = h_{j+1} + slope_{j+1} * (z - x_{j+1})
  #   z = (h_{j+1} - h_j + slope_j * x_j - slope_{j+1} * x_{j+1}) / (slope_j - slope_{j+1})

  if (k < 2) stop("Need at least 2 abscissae for upper hull.")

  # k-1 segments
  seg_slopes <- secant_slopes
  seg_intercepts <- h_vals[1:(k-1)] - seg_slopes * abscissae[1:(k-1)]
  # u_j(x) = seg_intercepts[j] + seg_slopes[j] * x

  # Intersection points z between consecutive segments
  # z[j] is where segment j meets segment j+1 (for j = 1,...,k-2)
  z_inner <- numeric(0)
  if (k > 2) {
    z_inner <- (seg_intercepts[2:(k-1)] - seg_intercepts[1:(k-2)]) /
               (seg_slopes[1:(k-2)] - seg_slopes[2:(k-1)])
  }

  # Full z vector including domain bounds
  z <- c(lb, z_inner, ub)

  return(list(
    z = z,
    seg_slopes = seg_slopes,
    seg_intercepts = seg_intercepts
  ))
}

#' Evaluate the upper hull at a point x
#' @param x point to evaluate
#' @param hull list from compute_upper_hull
#' @return u(x), the upper hull value
eval_upper_hull <- function(x, hull) {
  z <- hull$z
  seg_slopes <- hull$seg_slopes
  seg_intercepts <- hull$seg_intercepts
  n_seg <- length(seg_slopes)

  # Find which segment x falls in
  # Segment j covers [z[j], z[j+1]]
  seg_idx <- findInterval(x, z, rightmost.closed = TRUE)
  seg_idx <- pmax(1, pmin(seg_idx, n_seg))

  return(seg_intercepts[seg_idx] + seg_slopes[seg_idx] * x)
}

#' Evaluate the lower hull (piecewise linear through abscissae) at a point x
#' @param x point to evaluate
#' @param abscissae sorted x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding h(x) values
#' @return l(x), the lower hull value. -Inf if outside the range of abscissae.
eval_lower_hull <- function(x, abscissae, h_vals) {
  k <- length(abscissae)
  if (x <= abscissae[1] || x >= abscissae[k]) {
    return(-Inf)
  }
  # Find which interval x falls in
  j <- findInterval(x, abscissae)
  j <- pmax(1, pmin(j, k - 1))

  # Linear interpolation
  t <- (x - abscissae[j]) / (abscissae[j+1] - abscissae[j])
  return(h_vals[j] * (1 - t) + h_vals[j+1] * t)
}

#' Sample from the upper hull (piecewise-exponential distribution)
#' The upper hull is a piecewise linear function on log scale, which corresponds
#' to a piecewise exponential distribution.
#' @param n number of samples to draw
#' @param hull list from compute_upper_hull
#' @return vector of n samples from the upper hull distribution
sample_upper_hull <- function(n, hull) {
  z <- hull$z
  seg_slopes <- hull$seg_slopes
  seg_intercepts <- hull$seg_intercepts
  n_seg <- length(seg_slopes)

  # For each segment j on [z_j, z_{j+1}]:
  # The unnormalized density is exp(a_j + b_j * x) where a_j = seg_intercepts[j], b_j = seg_slopes[j]
  # The integral is:
  #   if b_j != 0: exp(a_j) * (exp(b_j * z_{j+1}) - exp(b_j * z_j)) / b_j
  #   if b_j == 0: exp(a_j) * (z_{j+1} - z_j)

  # Compute area under each segment
  areas <- numeric(n_seg)
  for (j in 1:n_seg) {
    lo <- z[j]
    hi <- z[j + 1]
    if (!is.finite(lo)) lo <- z[j + 1] - 10  # fallback for -Inf
    if (!is.finite(hi)) hi <- z[j] + 10       # fallback for +Inf
    a <- seg_intercepts[j]
    b <- seg_slopes[j]

    if (abs(b) < 1e-15) {
      areas[j] <- exp(a) * (hi - lo)
    } else {
      # Integral of exp(a + b*x) from lo to hi
      # = exp(a)/b * (exp(b*hi) - exp(b*lo))
      # Use log-sum-exp for numerical stability
      log_hi <- a + b * hi
      log_lo <- a + b * lo
      if (b > 0) {
        areas[j] <- exp(log_hi) * (1 - exp(log_lo - log_hi)) / b
      } else {
        areas[j] <- exp(log_lo) * (1 - exp(log_hi - log_lo)) / (-b)
      }
    }
    if (is.na(areas[j]) || !is.finite(areas[j]) || areas[j] < 0) {
      areas[j] <- 0
    }
  }

  total_area <- sum(areas)
  if (total_area <= 0 || !is.finite(total_area)) {
    stop("Upper hull has zero or infinite area. Check domain bounds.")
  }
  probs <- areas / total_area

  # Sample segments according to their areas
  seg_choices <- sample.int(n_seg, size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = probs)

  # For each chosen segment, sample from the truncated exponential
  samples <- numeric(n)
  for (i in 1:n) {
    j <- seg_choices[i]
    lo <- z[j]
    hi <- z[j + 1]
    if (!is.finite(lo)) lo <- z[j + 1] - 10
    if (!is.finite(hi)) hi <- z[j] + 10
    b <- seg_slopes[j]

    if (abs(b) < 1e-15) {
      # Uniform on [lo, hi]
      samples[i] <- runif(1, lo, hi)
    } else {
      # Inverse CDF of truncated exponential
      # CDF: F(x) = (exp(b*x) - exp(b*lo)) / (exp(b*hi) - exp(b*lo))
      u <- runif(1)
      # x = log(exp(b*lo) + u * (exp(b*hi) - exp(b*lo))) / b
      # Numerically stable version:
      log_lo <- b * lo
      log_hi <- b * hi
      if (b > 0) {
        samples[i] <- (log_lo + log(1 + u * (exp(log_hi - log_lo) - 1))) / b
      } else {
        samples[i] <- (log_hi + log(1 + (1 - u) * (exp(log_lo - log_hi) - 1))) / b
      }
    }
  }
  return(samples)
}

#' Choose initial abscissae for the ARS algorithm
#' @param lb lower bound
#' @param ub upper bound
#' @param f density function
#' @param n_init number of initial points (default 5)
#' @return vector of initial abscissae
choose_initial_abscissae <- function(lb, ub, f, n_init = 5) {
  # Handle infinite bounds
  if (is.finite(lb) && is.finite(ub)) {
    # Both finite: space evenly
    pts <- seq(lb + (ub - lb) * 0.05, ub - (ub - lb) * 0.05, length.out = n_init)
  } else if (is.finite(lb) && !is.finite(ub)) {
    pts <- lb + (1:n_init) * 1.0
  } else if (!is.finite(lb) && is.finite(ub)) {
    pts <- ub - rev((1:n_init) * 1.0)
  } else {
    # Both infinite: center around 0
    pts <- seq(-2, 2, length.out = n_init)
  }

  # Verify all points have positive density
  vals <- f(pts)
  if (any(is.na(vals) | vals <= 0)) {
    # Try to find good points by searching
    good <- which(vals > 0 & !is.na(vals))
    if (length(good) < 2) {
      stop("Cannot find initial abscissae with positive density. Check domain bounds.")
    }
    pts <- pts[good]
  }

  return(pts)
}

#' Validate inputs to the ars function
#' @param f density function
#' @param n number of samples
#' @param lb lower bound
#' @param ub upper bound
validate_inputs <- function(f, n, lb, ub) {
  if (!is.function(f)) {
    stop("'f' must be a function.")
  }
  if (!is.numeric(n) || length(n) != 1 || n < 1 || n != as.integer(n)) {
    stop("'n' must be a positive integer.")
  }
  if (!is.numeric(lb) || length(lb) != 1) {
    stop("'lb' must be a single numeric value.")
  }
  if (!is.numeric(ub) || length(ub) != 1) {
    stop("'ub' must be a single numeric value.")
  }
  if (lb >= ub) {
    stop("Lower bound must be strictly less than upper bound.")
  }
}

# --- Main ARS Function ---

#' Adaptive Rejection Sampling
#'
#' @param f density function (possibly unnormalized), must be log-concave.
#'          Should accept a vector and return a vector of density values.
#' @param support optional 2-element vector c(lb, ub) for the domain
#' @param n number of samples to generate (default 100)
#' @param lb lower bound of the domain (default -Inf)
#' @param ub upper bound of the domain (default Inf)
#' @param ... additional arguments (currently unused)
#' @return vector of n samples from the target distribution
ars <- function(f, support = NULL, n = 100, lb = -Inf, ub = Inf, ...) {
  # Handle the support vector argument (alternative call pattern)
  if (!is.null(support) && is.numeric(support) && length(support) == 2) {
    lb <- support[1]
    ub <- support[2]
  }

  # Validate inputs
  validate_inputs(f, n, lb, ub)

  # Choose initial abscissae
  abscissae <- choose_initial_abscissae(lb, ub, f)
  h_vals <- eval_log_density(f, abscissae)

  # Check initial log-concavity
  check_log_concavity(abscissae, h_vals)

  # Main sampling loop
  samples <- numeric(n)
  count <- 0
  max_iter <- n * 100  # safety limit to avoid infinite loops
  iter <- 0

  while (count < n && iter < max_iter) {
    iter <- iter + 1

    # Build upper hull
    hull <- compute_upper_hull(abscissae, h_vals, lb, ub)

    # Sample from upper hull
    x_star <- sample_upper_hull(1, hull)

    # Squeeze test
    u <- runif(1)
    u_val <- eval_upper_hull(x_star, hull)
    l_val <- eval_lower_hull(x_star, abscissae, h_vals)

    if (u <= exp(l_val - u_val)) {
      # Accept by squeeze test (no density evaluation needed)
      count <- count + 1
      samples[count] <- x_star
    } else {
      # Need to evaluate the true log-density
      h_star <- eval_log_density(f, x_star)

      if (u <= exp(h_star - u_val)) {
        # Accept by rejection test
        count <- count + 1
        samples[count] <- x_star
      }

      # Update the abscissae with the new point
      insert_pos <- findInterval(x_star, abscissae) + 1
      abscissae <- append(abscissae, x_star, after = insert_pos - 1)
      h_vals <- append(h_vals, h_star, after = insert_pos - 1)

      # Re-check log-concavity with new point
      check_log_concavity(abscissae, h_vals)
    }
  }

  if (count < n) {
    warning(paste("Only generated", count, "out of", n, "requested samples."))
  }

  return(samples)
}

# --- Test Function ---

#' Run formal tests of the ARS implementation
#' Compares samples against known distributions using statistical tests
test <- function() {
  cat("============================================================\n")
  cat("     Adaptive Rejection Sampling (ARS) - Test Suite\n")
  cat("============================================================\n\n")

  pass_count <- 0
  fail_count <- 0

  run_test <- function(name, expr) {
    result <- tryCatch(expr, error = function(e) {
      cat(paste0("  Error: ", e$message, "\n"))
      return(FALSE)
    })
    if (isTRUE(result)) {
      cat(paste0(name, ": PASS\n\n"))
      pass_count <<- pass_count + 1
    } else {
      cat(paste0(name, ": FAIL\n\n"))
      fail_count <<- fail_count + 1
    }
  }

  # ---- Input Validation Tests ----
  cat("--- Input Validation Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_negative_n", {
    cat("  Testing that negative n is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = -5); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("positive integer", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_zero_n", {
    cat("  Testing that n=0 is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = 0); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("positive integer", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_non_integer_n", {
    cat("  Testing that non-integer n is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = 3.5); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("positive integer", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_invalid_bounds", {
    cat("  Testing that lb >= ub is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = 10, lb = 5, ub = 2); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("strictly less", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_non_function_f", {
    cat("  Testing that non-function f is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars("not_a_function", n = 10); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("must be a function", e$message))
    err
  })

  # ---- Log-Concavity Tests ----
  cat("--- Log-Concavity Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_bimodal_rejection", {
    cat("  Testing rejection of bimodal (non-log-concave) density...\n")
    bimodal <- function(x) 0.5 * dnorm(x, -3, 0.5) + 0.5 * dnorm(x, 3, 0.5)
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(bimodal, n = 1000, lb = -10, ub = 10); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("[Ll]og-concav", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_cauchy_rejection", {
    cat("  Testing rejection of Cauchy (non-log-concave on wide domain)...\n")
    # Cauchy is NOT log-concave: log(1/(1+x^2)) = -log(1+x^2) has
    # second derivative (x^2 - 1)/(1+x^2)^2, which is positive for |x|>1
    # However, Cauchy IS log-concave. Let me use t-distribution with df=1.
    # Actually, Cauchy = t(df=1). log f(x) = -log(1+x^2) (up to constant)
    # d^2/dx^2 [-log(1+x^2)] = (2x^2-2)/(1+x^2)^2 which changes sign.
    # So Cauchy IS log-concave only near the center.
    # On a wide enough domain with enough initial points, we should catch it.
    # Actually, the second derivative of -log(1+x^2) is (2(x^2-1))/(1+x^2)^2
    # which is negative for |x| < 1 and positive for |x| > 1.
    # So Cauchy is NOT log-concave. Good.
    err <- tryCatch({
      ars(dcauchy, n = 1000, lb = -100, ub = 100)
      FALSE
    }, error = function(e) grepl("[Ll]og-concav", e$message))
    err
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Standard Normal) ----
  cat("--- Standard Normal Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_normal_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 5000 points from N(0,1)...\n")
    set.seed(42)
    samples <- ars(n = 5000, f = dnorm, lb = -Inf, ub = Inf)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~0)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))

    # Save samples
    writeLines(as.character(samples), "/app/normal_samples.txt")
    cat("  Saved samples to /app/normal_samples.txt\n")

    # KS test against N(0,1)
    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pnorm")
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    # Check: mean close to 0, sd close to 1, KS p-value > 0.01
    abs(mean(samples)) < 0.15 && abs(sd(samples) - 1) < 0.15 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  run_test("test_normal_bounded", {
    cat("  Sampling 2000 points from N(0,1) on [-5, 5]...\n")
    set.seed(123)
    samples <- ars(dnorm, c(-5, 5), n = 2000)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), "\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), "\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Min: ", round(min(samples), 4), ", Max: ", round(max(samples), 4), "\n"))

    # All samples should be within bounds
    all(samples >= -5 & samples <= 5) && abs(mean(samples)) < 0.2
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Exponential) ----
  cat("--- Exponential Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_exponential_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 5000 points from Exp(1) on [0, 100]...\n")
    set.seed(99)
    samples <- ars(dexp, c(0.001, 100), n = 5000)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))

    # Save samples
    writeLines(as.character(samples), "/app/exponential_samples.txt")
    cat("  Saved samples to /app/exponential_samples.txt\n")

    # KS test
    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pexp")
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples) - 1) < 0.15 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Gamma) ----
  cat("--- Gamma Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_gamma_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 3000 points from Gamma(3, 1) on [0.01, 30]...\n")
    set.seed(77)
    gamma_dens <- function(x) dgamma(x, shape = 3, rate = 1)
    samples <- ars(gamma_dens, c(0.01, 30), n = 3000)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~3)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1.73)\n"))

    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pgamma", shape = 3, rate = 1)
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples) - 3) < 0.3 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Beta) ----
  cat("--- Beta Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_beta_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 3000 points from Beta(2, 5) on [0.01, 0.99]...\n")
    set.seed(55)
    beta_dens <- function(x) dbeta(x, shape1 = 2, shape2 = 5)
    samples <- ars(beta_dens, c(0.01, 0.99), n = 3000)
    expected_mean <- 2 / (2 + 5)
    expected_sd <- sqrt(2 * 5 / ((2 + 5)^2 * (2 + 5 + 1)))
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4),
               " (expected ~", round(expected_mean, 4), ")\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4),
               " (expected ~", round(expected_sd, 4), ")\n"))

    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pbeta", shape1 = 2, shape2 = 5)
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples) - expected_mean) < 0.05 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Log-Normal via unnormalized density) ----
  cat("--- Unnormalized Density Test ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_unnormalized_density", {
    cat("  Sampling from unnormalized Gaussian: f(x) = exp(-x^2/2)...\n")
    set.seed(33)
    unnorm_gauss <- function(x) exp(-x^2 / 2)
    samples <- ars(unnorm_gauss, n = 3000, lb = -Inf, ub = Inf)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~0)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))

    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pnorm")
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples)) < 0.15 && abs(sd(samples) - 1) < 0.15 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Module-Level Tests ----
  cat("--- Module-Level Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_eval_log_density", {
    cat("  Testing eval_log_density with dnorm...\n")
    result <- eval_log_density(dnorm, 0)
    expected <- log(dnorm(0))
    cat(paste0("  log(dnorm(0)) = ", round(result, 6), " (expected ", round(expected, 6), ")\n"))
    abs(result - expected) < 1e-10
  })

  run_test("test_check_log_concavity_valid", {
    cat("  Testing log-concavity check on concave data...\n")
    # h(x) = -x^2 is concave
    x <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)
    h <- -x^2
    result <- tryCatch({ check_log_concavity(x, h); TRUE },
                       error = function(e) FALSE)
    result
  })

  run_test("test_check_log_concavity_invalid", {
    cat("  Testing log-concavity check on convex data...\n")
    # h(x) = x^2 is convex (NOT concave)
    x <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)
    h <- x^2
    result <- tryCatch({ check_log_concavity(x, h); FALSE },
                       error = function(e) grepl("[Ll]og-concav", e$message))
    result
  })

  run_test("test_upper_hull_above_density", {
    cat("  Testing that upper hull is above log-density at test points...\n")
    set.seed(11)
    x_abs <- c(-3, -1, 0, 1, 3)
    h_abs <- eval_log_density(dnorm, x_abs)
    hull <- compute_upper_hull(x_abs, h_abs, -Inf, Inf)

    # Check at many random points
    test_pts <- seq(-2.5, 2.5, by = 0.1)
    h_test <- eval_log_density(dnorm, test_pts)
    u_test <- eval_upper_hull(test_pts, hull)

    all_above <- all(u_test >= h_test - 1e-10)
    cat(paste0("  Upper hull >= log-density at all ", length(test_pts), " test points: ", all_above, "\n"))
    all_above
  })

  run_test("test_lower_hull_below_density", {
    cat("  Testing that lower hull is below log-density at test points...\n")
    x_abs <- c(-3, -1, 0, 1, 3)
    h_abs <- eval_log_density(dnorm, x_abs)

    test_pts <- seq(-2.5, 2.5, by = 0.1)
    h_test <- eval_log_density(dnorm, test_pts)
    l_test <- sapply(test_pts, function(x) eval_lower_hull(x, x_abs, h_abs))

    all_below <- all(l_test <= h_test + 1e-10)
    cat(paste0("  Lower hull <= log-density at all ", length(test_pts), " test points: ", all_below, "\n"))
    all_below
  })

  # ---- Summary ----
  cat("============================================================\n")
  cat(paste0("  TOTAL: ", pass_count + fail_count, " tests, ",
             pass_count, " passed, ", fail_count, " failed\n"))
  cat("============================================================\n")

  invisible(list(passed = pass_count, failed = fail_count))
}

# Run tests if this script is executed directly
if (sys.nframe() == 0) {
  test()
}

steps.reasoning_content:
I have several issues:

1. **Exponential sampling fails** with "vec must be sorted non-decreasingly" - likely because sample_upper_hull generates NaN values or the abscissae get out of order.

2. **Gamma and Beta KS test failures** - the KS test p-values are very low, meaning the samples don't match the distribution well.

3. **Upper hull not always above density** - my upper hull construction may be wrong.

The core issue is likely in my upper hull construction. In Gilks & Wild (1992), the upper hull is constructed using tangent lines, not secant lines. Since we don't have derivatives, the upper hull uses the secant lines between consecutive points, but the pieces of the upper hull are defined differently than what I implemented.


Let me reconsider the ARS algorithm from first principles. The key insight is that the lower hull is the piecewise linear interpolant through the log-density points, while the upper hull is constructed from secant line slopes between consecutive abscissae. I need to carefully review how the upper hull segments are actually defined and ensure they're always above the true density. The upper hull uses tangent lines at each abscissa point, where the tangent at x_j is defined as h(x_j) + h'(x_j) * (x - x_j). Since h is concave, these tangent lines sit above the function everywhere, and their minimum forms the upper envelope. For a derivative-free implementation, I can approximate h'(x_j) using secant slopes between consecutive points, bounded by log-concavity constraints. I'm realizing the derivative-free approach uses secant lines between non-adjacent points to form the upper hull. For a concave function, the secant from x_j to x_{j+1} provides an upper bound in the region between consecutive intersection points, with the leftmost region using the secant from x_1 to x_2 and the rightmost region following a similar pattern. I'm realizing those two secant lines both pass through the same point at x_j, so they can't form an upper hull. I need to reconsider the approach—Gilks & Wild's method relies on derivatives, so I should either numerically approximate them or find an alternative construction for the upper hull without explicit derivatives. The upper hull is defined as the minimum of all these tangent lines across the appropriate interval. To find where two adjacent tangent lines intersect, I solve for the point z where u_j and u_{j+1} are equal, which gives me a formula in terms of the function values and derivatives at those points. Since I don't have explicit derivatives, I can approximate them using secant slopes between consecutive points, constructing lines that stay above the function.

For the endpoints, I use the secant slopes directly, but for interior points, I take the intersection of adjacent secant lines. Actually, I'm realizing there's a sign issue here—secant lines of a concave function lie below it, not above, so I need to reconsider whether I'm building the upper or lower hull correctly.

Let me think through the upper hull construction more carefully. Since the function is log-concave, the slopes are decreasing: s_1 > s_2 > ... > s_{k-1}. The tangent at any point x_j satisfies s_{j-1} ≥ h'(x_j) ≥ s_j, which means the secant line through (x_1, x_2) with slope s_1 should lie above the function for x < x_1 since that slope is steeper than any tangent to the left.

But wait, I need to reconsider this. Using the tangent line at x_1 with slope h'(x_1), concavity gives h(x) ≤ h(x_1) + h'(x_1)(x - x_1). If I replace h'(x_1) with the larger slope s_1, then for x < x_1 where (x - x_1) is negative, multiplying by the larger slope s_1 actually makes the product smaller, so the line with slope s_1 ends up below the tangent line. This means the secant line is actually below the function, not above it—so I need to reconsider which line forms the upper hull.

For a concave function on an interval between two points, the tangent lines at the endpoints are above the function, and the maximum of these two tangents gives me the upper bound I need.

Since I don't have explicit tangent slopes, I can use the secant line from the previous point instead—the slope from x_{j-1} to x_j, when extended past x_j, stays above h on the interval [x_j, x_{j+1}] because that secant slope is at least as steep as the tangent at x_j, and a line through x_j with that slope will be above the concave function.

By the same logic, the secant from x_{j+1} to x_{j+2}, extended backward to x < x_{j+1}, also stays above h. So the upper hull on [x_j, x_{j+1}] is the minimum of these two lines—the one from the left and the one from the right. j) with slope s_{j-1} (the secant from the LEFT of x_j)
- Line through (x_{j+1}, h_{j+1}) with slope s_{j+1} (the secant from the RIGHT of x_{j+1})

And the intersection of these two lines gives the breakpoint z_j.

For the boundary cases:
- For x < x_1: use the line through (x_1, h_1) with slope s_1 (secant to the RIGHT of x_1). Since s_1 <= h'(x_1), for x < x_1: h(x_1) + s_1(x-x_1) >= h(x_1) + h'(x_1)(x-x_1) >= h(x) by concavity. ✓

For x > x_k: use the line through (x_k, h_k) with slope s_{k-1} (secant to the LEFT of x_k). Since s_{k-1} >= h'(x_k), for x > x_k we get h_k + s_{k-1}(x-x_k) >= h_k + h'(x_k)(x-x_k) >= h(x). ✓

So the upper hull is built from segments where each interior segment uses the left secant slope on the left side and right secant slope on the right side, with breakpoints determined by line intersections.

But I'm realizing the boundary case at x_1 is tricky—there's no secant to the left of the leftmost point, so I need to rethink how the construction actually works. Let me step back and reconsider the whole approach: for each abscissa x_j, I should construct a tangent line, but without explicit derivatives available, I need to use secants instead. For each point x_j, I need to determine which slope to use for the tangent line—at the boundaries I use the adjacent secant slopes, but for interior points I'm considering whether to take the left or right secant slope, or perhaps construct something between them. Let me step back and look at how the actual algorithm builds the upper hull as a piecewise linear function. I'm realizing the approach with extending lines from adjacent intervals is getting complicated, especially at the boundaries. Let me think about this more carefully—there should be a cleaner way to define the upper hull using just the secant slopes. Let me reconsider.

The key insight in the derivative-free ARS is:

We have k abscissae and k-1 secant slopes s_1 > s_2 > ... > s_{k-1}.

The upper hull is constructed by extending each secant line BEYOND its interval:
- Line j (passing through x_j and x_{j+1} with slope s_j) extended to all x
- For j < j': L_j(x) > L_{j'}(x) for x sufficiently large (because s_j > s_{j'})
- For j > j': L_j(x) > L_{j'}(x) for x sufficiently small

The upper hull is u(x) = min_j L_j(x), which is a piecewise linear concave function that is above h(x) everywhere.

But wait—I need to verify this actually works. On the interval [x_j, x_{j+1}], the secant line L_j passes through both endpoints, so by concavity of h it actually lies below h on that interval. Taking the minimum of all these lines would make it even lower, not higher. That can't be right for an upper hull.

I think the real issue is that derivative-free ARS must use tangent lines instead of secants to construct a proper upper bound, but tangent lines require knowing derivatives. Let me reconsider the original Gilks & Wild approach. So the secant slope from the left, s_{j-1}, provides a valid upper bound for x > x_j since it's steeper than the actual derivative, and similarly the secant slope from the right, s_j, bounds the function for x < x_j since it's less steep than the derivative at that point.

Now I'm constructing the upper hull by taking the minimum of these left and right secant lines for each interval between consecutive points, and I need to define the tangent line formulas explicitly for the boundary cases and interior points. I'm realizing the intersection approach gets messy at the boundaries, so maybe I should just compute numerical derivatives using a small epsilon instead. Or better yet, follow the original Gilks & Wild approach where the user provides both h(x) and its derivative directly. I'm verifying that the upper hull pieces work correctly at the boundaries—using the right secant slope when x is less than x_j and the left secant slope when x is greater than x_j ensures the bound stays tight against the tangent line. For the leftmost region before x_1, I only have the right secant available. I'm getting tangled up on the direction of these secant slopes and what they bound. Let me reconsider: for a concave function, the secant between two points lies below the function, so I need to think carefully about which secants give upper bounds at different positions.

At each point x_j, I have two secants available—one from the left and one from the right—and their slopes relate to the derivative in opposite ways depending on which endpoint I'm considering. For the tangent-based upper hull, I need to figure out which secant slope actually provides a valid upper bound at each interior point. Using the left slope s_{j-1} works as an upper bound to the right of x_j but fails to the left, while using the right slope s_j does the opposite—works to the left but fails to the right. So neither single tangent line can serve as a global upper bound.

The key insight is that I can construct the upper hull by taking the minimum of two tangent lines over each interval [x_j, x_{j+1}]: one extending leftward from x_j with slope s_{j-1} (valid for x ≥ x_j) and one extending rightward from x_{j+1} with slope s_{j+1} (valid for x ≤ x_{j+1}). Since both are above the curve, their minimum is also above it. At the boundaries, I need to handle the special case where there's no left slope available at x_1. For the regions outside the interval, I need to verify the upper bound conditions. On the left side of x_1, the line from x_1 with slope s_1 works because the slope is less than or equal to the derivative at x_1, so when multiplied by the negative distance, it gives an upper bound. Similarly, on the right side beyond x_k, the line from x_k with slope s_{k-1} should provide the upper bound, though the argument appears to be cut off.

Now I'm working through the structure of the correct upper hull: for k points, there are k-1 secant slopes that form the pieces. Outside the interval, I use lines from the endpoints with appropriate slopes, and within each interval between consecutive points, I need to piece together the left and right sub-pieces using the secant slopes from adjacent intervals. I see the structure now—each interior point has both left and right tangent lines with their respective slopes, while the boundary points only have one tangent each. Let me code this up to compute the upper hull pieces systematically. 2. For z_{j-1} <= x <= z_j: use x_j's tangent 
   - To the left of x_j: right tangent (slope s_j)
   - To the right of x_j: left tangent (slope s_{j-1})
   Wait, but these give different values at x_j itself:
   Right tangent at x_j: h_j + s_j * 0 = h_j
   Left tangent at x_j: h_j + s_{j-1} * 0 = h_j
   OK they agree at x_j.
   
   But this means each x_j has a "tent" shape: line going left with slope s_j, line going right with slope s_{j-1}. Since s_{j-1} > s_j (by concavity), this forms a concave tent.
   
3. For x >= z_{k-1}: use x_k's left tangent (slope s_{k-1})

Now I need to find where adjacent tents intersect. Setting the right-extending line from x_j equal to the left-extending line from x_{j+1} and solving for their intersection point z.

For j = 1, I need to reconsider since there's no x_0 to provide a left tangent. The first point x_1 only has its right tangent with slope s_1 extending leftward.

For j = 2 and beyond, each point has both a right tangent (using the next secant slope) extending left and a left tangent (using the previous secant slope) extending right. I'm working through how these tangent lines intersect to form the piecewise linear upper envelope. At x_j, I can construct tangent lines using either secant slope. The left secant s_{j-1} overestimates the true derivative, so a line through x_j with that slope gives an upper bound to the right and a lower bound to the left. The right secant s_j underestimates the derivative, creating the opposite behavior.

This means at each point x_j, I can form a "tent" shape by taking the right secant slope for x ≤ x_j and switching to the left secant slope for x ≥ x_j—both pieces serve as valid upper bounds. The upper hull itself is then the minimum of all these tent functions across the domain.

Between adjacent tents, they intersect at specific points where the right arm of one tent meets the left arm of the next, which I can calculate using the slopes and function values. At the boundary points like x_1, there's no left secant available, so only the right arm of that tent contributes to the hull. The upper hull alternates between these right and left arm segments as we move through the points.

So I need to find where each pair of consecutive arms intersect—that's the z_j values. The intersection points z_j are determined by where consecutive tents meet, and I'm starting to work through the algebra for the first intersection.

Now I'm deriving the general formula for each intersection point z_j by setting the right side of tent j equal to the left side of tent j+1, which gives me a formula involving the heights and slopes of adjacent tents.

I'm realizing there's a more elegant way to frame this: I have k tangent lines, each anchored at a point (x_j, h_j) with a specific slope, and I need to find where consecutive lines intersect. s_{k-1}. At interior x_j, the tangent is a "tent" with two slopes (s_j on the left, s_{j-1} on the right).

I can flatten this into a list of line segments:

Lines (from left to right):
Line 1: through (x_1, h_1), slope s_1
Line 2: through (x_2, h_2), slope s_2
Line 3: through (x_2, h_2), slope s_1
Line 4: through (x_3, h_3), slope s_3
Line 5: through (x_3, h_3), slope s_2
...
Line 2j-2: through (x_j, h_j), slope s_j
Line 2j-1: through (x_j, h_j), slope s_{j-1}

The breakpoints between consecutive lines alternate between intersection points and the data points themselves—z_1 separates the first two lines, then x_2 where they meet, then z_2 for the next intersection, and so on, giving me the full sequence: lb, z_1, x_2, z_2, x_3, z_3, ..., x_k.

Now I'm considering whether to implement this exact piecewise construction or use a simpler numerical approach. Computing numerical derivatives with a small epsilon would let me use the standard Gilks & Wild algorithm directly, which is cleaner. The trade-off is that numerical derivatives might introduce tiny errors—the tangent line could be slightly below the true one—but with careful epsilon selection, this should be negligible in practice.

Actually, I think the secant-based approach is worth doing properly. Let me rewrite the upper hull computation cleanly...

Implementing the upper hull function... Building the line segments for the upper hull... Building the segment arrays... Finishing the breakpoint calculation and converting to intercept form...

Now I'm realizing that for k=2, the secant line actually represents the lower hull itself.  Since the function is concave, using that secant as an upper hull would incorrectly reject valid samples. I need to either require at least three initial points, or handle the k=2 case specially by constructing tangent lines from the endpoints instead of relying on secants alone. x_1 with slope s_1, extending left (valid for x <= x_1) → but extends right too, goes above h eventually for x far right
   - Line through x_2 with slope s_1, extending right (valid for x >= x_2) → but extends left too
   
   Actually for k=2 we only have one secant slope s_1. The "tent" from the correct approach would be:
   - For x <= x_1: h_1 + s_1(x - x_1), which is above h ✓
   - For x_1 <= x <= x_2: the secant h_1 + s_1(x - x_1), which is BELOW h ✗
   - For x >= x_2: h_2 + s_1(x - x_2), which is above h ✓

The issue is that with only two points, the middle region between them has no valid upper bound. The standard fix is to initialize with at least three abscissae instead. With k=3 and s_1 > s_2, I can construct proper segments: the first slope handles the left tail, the second slope covers the middle region, and the first slope again handles the right tail, creating a valid piecewise linear upper hull.

I see the core problem now—I was treating secant lines as upper bounds when they're actually lower bounds for concave functions. The correct approach uses extended secant lines positioned to form a proper upper envelope. Let me rebuild this implementation from scratch with the right geometry.

I also need to verify the tail behavior carefully: when the domain extends to infinity, the upper hull slopes must point downward in those directions so the integral stays finite. If the left slope is non-positive with an unbounded left domain, or the right slope is non-negative with an unbounded right domain, the integral diverges and the method breaks down.

steps.message: I see several issues with the upper hull construction. The secant lines of a concave function lie BELOW it, so my upper hull was actually below the density in some regions. I need to rewrite with the correct derivative-free upper hull construction from Gilks & Wild. Let me fix this.

tool_use_result.originalFile:
###############################################################################
# Adaptive Rejection Sampling (ARS)
# Based on Gilks, W.R. & Wild, P. (1992)
# "Adaptive Rejection Sampling for Gibbs Sampling"
# Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series C, 41(2), 337-348.
###############################################################################

# --- Auxiliary / Module Functions ---

#' Evaluate the log of a density function safely
#' @param f density function (possibly unnormalized)
#' @param x point(s) at which to evaluate
#' @return log(f(x)), with protection against f(x) <= 0
eval_log_density <- function(f, x) {
  fx <- f(x)
  if (any(is.na(fx) | is.nan(fx))) {
    stop("Density function returned NA/NaN.")
  }
  if (any(fx < 0)) {
    stop("Density function returned negative value.")
  }
  result <- rep(-Inf, length(fx))
  pos <- fx > 0
  result[pos] <- log(fx[pos])
  return(result)
}

#' Check log-concavity at three consecutive abscissae
#' If h(x) = log(f(x)), then log-concavity requires:
#'   (h(x2) - h(x1))/(x2 - x1) >= (h(x3) - h(x2))/(x3 - x2)
#' for all x1 < x2 < x3
#' @param abscissae sorted vector of x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding log-density values h(x)
#' @return TRUE if log-concave, else stops with error
check_log_concavity <- function(abscissae, h_vals) {
  n <- length(abscissae)
  if (n < 3) return(TRUE)
  for (i in 1:(n - 2)) {
    x1 <- abscissae[i];   h1 <- h_vals[i]
    x2 <- abscissae[i+1]; h2 <- h_vals[i+1]
    x3 <- abscissae[i+2]; h3 <- h_vals[i+2]
    slope_left  <- (h2 - h1) / (x2 - x1)
    slope_right <- (h3 - h2) / (x3 - x2)
    if (slope_right > slope_left + 1e-10) {
      stop(paste0(
        "Log-concavity violated at x = ", x2,
        ". Left slope = ", round(slope_left, 6),
        ", Right slope = ", round(slope_right, 6),
        ". The density may not be log-concave."
      ))
    }
  }
  return(TRUE)
}

#' Compute the slopes between consecutive abscissae on the log-density
#' @param abscissae sorted x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding h(x)
#' @return vector of slopes of length (n-1)
compute_slopes <- function(abscissae, h_vals) {
  n <- length(abscissae)
  slopes <- (h_vals[2:n] - h_vals[1:(n-1)]) / (abscissae[2:n] - abscissae[1:(n-1)])
  return(slopes)
}

#' Compute the upper hull (piecewise-linear envelope)
#' The upper hull at each abscissa j uses the tangent lines from adjacent segments.
#' For j=1: use slope from segment (x1, x2)
#' For j=k (last): use slope from segment (x_{k-1}, x_k)
#' For interior j: the tangent from left segment and right segment
#'
#' Returns the intersection points z between consecutive tangent lines,
#' plus the slopes and intercepts of each tangent segment.
#'
#' @param abscissae sorted x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding h(x)
#' @param lb lower bound of domain
#' @param ub upper bound of domain
#' @return list with z (intersection points), slopes, intercepts for upper hull
compute_upper_hull <- function(abscissae, h_vals, lb, ub) {
  k <- length(abscissae)

  # Slopes of secant lines between consecutive abscissae
  secant_slopes <- compute_slopes(abscissae, h_vals)

  # Each tangent line j: u_j(x) = h_j + s_j * (x - x_j)
  # where s_j is the slope assigned to abscissa j
  # For the upper hull in ARS:
  #   - segment j uses the line through (x_j, h_j) and (x_{j+1}, h_{j+1})
  #   i.e., slope = secant_slope[j], intercept at x_j
  # There are k-1 segments. The hull extends to lb on the left and ub on the right.

  # The upper hull consists of k-1 line segments connecting consecutive points.
  # But in ARS, the upper hull is defined by tangent lines at each abscissa.
  # For log-concave densities, the tangent at x_j has slope between
  # the left secant and right secant.

  # Actually, in Gilks & Wild (1992), the upper hull u_k(x) is the
  # minimum of the tangent lines. With only function values (no derivatives),
  # they use the secant lines. Specifically:
  #
  # The upper hull is piecewise linear with pieces defined by:
  #   piece j (for j = 1, ..., k-1): line through (x_j, h_j) and (x_{j+1}, h_{j+1})
  #
  # The intersection z_j of piece j and piece j+1 gives the breakpoints.

  # For j = 1,...,k-1, line j has:
  #   slope_j = (h_{j+1} - h_j) / (x_{j+1} - x_j)
  #   u_j(x) = h_j + slope_j * (x - x_j)
  #
  # Intersection of line j and line j+1:
  #   h_j + slope_j * (z - x_j) = h_{j+1} + slope_{j+1} * (z - x_{j+1})
  #   z = (h_{j+1} - h_j + slope_j * x_j - slope_{j+1} * x_{j+1}) / (slope_j - slope_{j+1})

  if (k < 2) stop("Need at least 2 abscissae for upper hull.")

  # k-1 segments
  seg_slopes <- secant_slopes
  seg_intercepts <- h_vals[1:(k-1)] - seg_slopes * abscissae[1:(k-1)]
  # u_j(x) = seg_intercepts[j] + seg_slopes[j] * x

  # Intersection points z between consecutive segments
  # z[j] is where segment j meets segment j+1 (for j = 1,...,k-2)
  z_inner <- numeric(0)
  if (k > 2) {
    z_inner <- (seg_intercepts[2:(k-1)] - seg_intercepts[1:(k-2)]) /
               (seg_slopes[1:(k-2)] - seg_slopes[2:(k-1)])
  }

  # Full z vector including domain bounds
  z <- c(lb, z_inner, ub)

  return(list(
    z = z,
    seg_slopes = seg_slopes,
    seg_intercepts = seg_intercepts
  ))
}

#' Evaluate the upper hull at a point x
#' @param x point to evaluate
#' @param hull list from compute_upper_hull
#' @return u(x), the upper hull value
eval_upper_hull <- function(x, hull) {
  z <- hull$z
  seg_slopes <- hull$seg_slopes
  seg_intercepts <- hull$seg_intercepts
  n_seg <- length(seg_slopes)

  # Find which segment x falls in
  # Segment j covers [z[j], z[j+1]]
  seg_idx <- findInterval(x, z, rightmost.closed = TRUE)
  seg_idx <- pmax(1, pmin(seg_idx, n_seg))

  return(seg_intercepts[seg_idx] + seg_slopes[seg_idx] * x)
}

#' Evaluate the lower hull (piecewise linear through abscissae) at a point x
#' @param x point to evaluate
#' @param abscissae sorted x values
#' @param h_vals corresponding h(x) values
#' @return l(x), the lower hull value. -Inf if outside the range of abscissae.
eval_lower_hull <- function(x, abscissae, h_vals) {
  k <- length(abscissae)
  if (x <= abscissae[1] || x >= abscissae[k]) {
    return(-Inf)
  }
  # Find which interval x falls in
  j <- findInterval(x, abscissae)
  j <- pmax(1, pmin(j, k - 1))

  # Linear interpolation
  t <- (x - abscissae[j]) / (abscissae[j+1] - abscissae[j])
  return(h_vals[j] * (1 - t) + h_vals[j+1] * t)
}

#' Sample from the upper hull (piecewise-exponential distribution)
#' The upper hull is a piecewise linear function on log scale, which corresponds
#' to a piecewise exponential distribution.
#' @param n number of samples to draw
#' @param hull list from compute_upper_hull
#' @return vector of n samples from the upper hull distribution
sample_upper_hull <- function(n, hull) {
  z <- hull$z
  seg_slopes <- hull$seg_slopes
  seg_intercepts <- hull$seg_intercepts
  n_seg <- length(seg_slopes)

  # For each segment j on [z_j, z_{j+1}]:
  # The unnormalized density is exp(a_j + b_j * x) where a_j = seg_intercepts[j], b_j = seg_slopes[j]
  # The integral is:
  #   if b_j != 0: exp(a_j) * (exp(b_j * z_{j+1}) - exp(b_j * z_j)) / b_j
  #   if b_j == 0: exp(a_j) * (z_{j+1} - z_j)

  # Compute area under each segment
  areas <- numeric(n_seg)
  for (j in 1:n_seg) {
    lo <- z[j]
    hi <- z[j + 1]
    if (!is.finite(lo)) lo <- z[j + 1] - 10  # fallback for -Inf
    if (!is.finite(hi)) hi <- z[j] + 10       # fallback for +Inf
    a <- seg_intercepts[j]
    b <- seg_slopes[j]

    if (abs(b) < 1e-15) {
      areas[j] <- exp(a) * (hi - lo)
    } else {
      # Integral of exp(a + b*x) from lo to hi
      # = exp(a)/b * (exp(b*hi) - exp(b*lo))
      # Use log-sum-exp for numerical stability
      log_hi <- a + b * hi
      log_lo <- a + b * lo
      if (b > 0) {
        areas[j] <- exp(log_hi) * (1 - exp(log_lo - log_hi)) / b
      } else {
        areas[j] <- exp(log_lo) * (1 - exp(log_hi - log_lo)) / (-b)
      }
    }
    if (is.na(areas[j]) || !is.finite(areas[j]) || areas[j] < 0) {
      areas[j] <- 0
    }
  }

  total_area <- sum(areas)
  if (total_area <= 0 || !is.finite(total_area)) {
    stop("Upper hull has zero or infinite area. Check domain bounds.")
  }
  probs <- areas / total_area

  # Sample segments according to their areas
  seg_choices <- sample.int(n_seg, size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = probs)

  # For each chosen segment, sample from the truncated exponential
  samples <- numeric(n)
  for (i in 1:n) {
    j <- seg_choices[i]
    lo <- z[j]
    hi <- z[j + 1]
    if (!is.finite(lo)) lo <- z[j + 1] - 10
    if (!is.finite(hi)) hi <- z[j] + 10
    b <- seg_slopes[j]

    if (abs(b) < 1e-15) {
      # Uniform on [lo, hi]
      samples[i] <- runif(1, lo, hi)
    } else {
      # Inverse CDF of truncated exponential
      # CDF: F(x) = (exp(b*x) - exp(b*lo)) / (exp(b*hi) - exp(b*lo))
      u <- runif(1)
      # x = log(exp(b*lo) + u * (exp(b*hi) - exp(b*lo))) / b
      # Numerically stable version:
      log_lo <- b * lo
      log_hi <- b * hi
      if (b > 0) {
        samples[i] <- (log_lo + log(1 + u * (exp(log_hi - log_lo) - 1))) / b
      } else {
        samples[i] <- (log_hi + log(1 + (1 - u) * (exp(log_lo - log_hi) - 1))) / b
      }
    }
  }
  return(samples)
}

#' Choose initial abscissae for the ARS algorithm
#' @param lb lower bound
#' @param ub upper bound
#' @param f density function
#' @param n_init number of initial points (default 5)
#' @return vector of initial abscissae
choose_initial_abscissae <- function(lb, ub, f, n_init = 5) {
  # Handle infinite bounds
  if (is.finite(lb) && is.finite(ub)) {
    # Both finite: space evenly
    pts <- seq(lb + (ub - lb) * 0.05, ub - (ub - lb) * 0.05, length.out = n_init)
  } else if (is.finite(lb) && !is.finite(ub)) {
    pts <- lb + (1:n_init) * 1.0
  } else if (!is.finite(lb) && is.finite(ub)) {
    pts <- ub - rev((1:n_init) * 1.0)
  } else {
    # Both infinite: center around 0
    pts <- seq(-2, 2, length.out = n_init)
  }

  # Verify all points have positive density
  vals <- f(pts)
  if (any(is.na(vals) | vals <= 0)) {
    # Try to find good points by searching
    good <- which(vals > 0 & !is.na(vals))
    if (length(good) < 2) {
      stop("Cannot find initial abscissae with positive density. Check domain bounds.")
    }
    pts <- pts[good]
  }

  return(pts)
}

#' Validate inputs to the ars function
#' @param f density function
#' @param n number of samples
#' @param lb lower bound
#' @param ub upper bound
validate_inputs <- function(f, n, lb, ub) {
  if (!is.function(f)) {
    stop("'f' must be a function.")
  }
  if (!is.numeric(n) || length(n) != 1 || n < 1 || n != as.integer(n)) {
    stop("'n' must be a positive integer.")
  }
  if (!is.numeric(lb) || length(lb) != 1) {
    stop("'lb' must be a single numeric value.")
  }
  if (!is.numeric(ub) || length(ub) != 1) {
    stop("'ub' must be a single numeric value.")
  }
  if (lb >= ub) {
    stop("Lower bound must be strictly less than upper bound.")
  }
}

# --- Main ARS Function ---

#' Adaptive Rejection Sampling
#'
#' @param f density function (possibly unnormalized), must be log-concave.
#'          Should accept a vector and return a vector of density values.
#' @param support optional 2-element vector c(lb, ub) for the domain
#' @param n number of samples to generate (default 100)
#' @param lb lower bound of the domain (default -Inf)
#' @param ub upper bound of the domain (default Inf)
#' @param ... additional arguments (currently unused)
#' @return vector of n samples from the target distribution
ars <- function(f, support = NULL, n = 100, lb = -Inf, ub = Inf, ...) {
  # Handle the support vector argument (alternative call pattern)
  if (!is.null(support) && is.numeric(support) && length(support) == 2) {
    lb <- support[1]
    ub <- support[2]
  }

  # Validate inputs
  validate_inputs(f, n, lb, ub)

  # Choose initial abscissae
  abscissae <- choose_initial_abscissae(lb, ub, f)
  h_vals <- eval_log_density(f, abscissae)

  # Check initial log-concavity
  check_log_concavity(abscissae, h_vals)

  # Main sampling loop
  samples <- numeric(n)
  count <- 0
  max_iter <- n * 100  # safety limit to avoid infinite loops
  iter <- 0

  while (count < n && iter < max_iter) {
    iter <- iter + 1

    # Build upper hull
    hull <- compute_upper_hull(abscissae, h_vals, lb, ub)

    # Sample from upper hull
    x_star <- sample_upper_hull(1, hull)

    # Squeeze test
    u <- runif(1)
    u_val <- eval_upper_hull(x_star, hull)
    l_val <- eval_lower_hull(x_star, abscissae, h_vals)

    if (u <= exp(l_val - u_val)) {
      # Accept by squeeze test (no density evaluation needed)
      count <- count + 1
      samples[count] <- x_star
    } else {
      # Need to evaluate the true log-density
      h_star <- eval_log_density(f, x_star)

      if (u <= exp(h_star - u_val)) {
        # Accept by rejection test
        count <- count + 1
        samples[count] <- x_star
      }

      # Update the abscissae with the new point
      insert_pos <- findInterval(x_star, abscissae) + 1
      abscissae <- append(abscissae, x_star, after = insert_pos - 1)
      h_vals <- append(h_vals, h_star, after = insert_pos - 1)

      # Re-check log-concavity with new point
      check_log_concavity(abscissae, h_vals)
    }
  }

  if (count < n) {
    warning(paste("Only generated", count, "out of", n, "requested samples."))
  }

  return(samples)
}

# --- Test Function ---

#' Run formal tests of the ARS implementation
#' Compares samples against known distributions using statistical tests
test <- function() {
  cat("============================================================\n")
  cat("     Adaptive Rejection Sampling (ARS) - Test Suite\n")
  cat("============================================================\n\n")

  pass_count <- 0
  fail_count <- 0

  run_test <- function(name, expr) {
    result <- tryCatch(expr, error = function(e) {
      cat(paste0("  Error: ", e$message, "\n"))
      return(FALSE)
    })
    if (isTRUE(result)) {
      cat(paste0(name, ": PASS\n\n"))
      pass_count <<- pass_count + 1
    } else {
      cat(paste0(name, ": FAIL\n\n"))
      fail_count <<- fail_count + 1
    }
  }

  # ---- Input Validation Tests ----
  cat("--- Input Validation Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_negative_n", {
    cat("  Testing that negative n is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = -5); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("positive integer", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_zero_n", {
    cat("  Testing that n=0 is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = 0); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("positive integer", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_non_integer_n", {
    cat("  Testing that non-integer n is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = 3.5); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("positive integer", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_invalid_bounds", {
    cat("  Testing that lb >= ub is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(dnorm, n = 10, lb = 5, ub = 2); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("strictly less", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_non_function_f", {
    cat("  Testing that non-function f is rejected...\n")
    err <- tryCatch({ ars("not_a_function", n = 10); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("must be a function", e$message))
    err
  })

  # ---- Log-Concavity Tests ----
  cat("--- Log-Concavity Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_bimodal_rejection", {
    cat("  Testing rejection of bimodal (non-log-concave) density...\n")
    bimodal <- function(x) 0.5 * dnorm(x, -3, 0.5) + 0.5 * dnorm(x, 3, 0.5)
    err <- tryCatch({ ars(bimodal, n = 1000, lb = -10, ub = 10); FALSE },
                    error = function(e) grepl("[Ll]og-concav", e$message))
    err
  })

  run_test("test_cauchy_rejection", {
    cat("  Testing rejection of Cauchy (non-log-concave on wide domain)...\n")
    # Cauchy is NOT log-concave: log(1/(1+x^2)) = -log(1+x^2) has
    # second derivative (x^2 - 1)/(1+x^2)^2, which is positive for |x|>1
    # However, Cauchy IS log-concave. Let me use t-distribution with df=1.
    # Actually, Cauchy = t(df=1). log f(x) = -log(1+x^2) (up to constant)
    # d^2/dx^2 [-log(1+x^2)] = (2x^2-2)/(1+x^2)^2 which changes sign.
    # So Cauchy IS log-concave only near the center.
    # On a wide enough domain with enough initial points, we should catch it.
    # Actually, the second derivative of -log(1+x^2) is (2(x^2-1))/(1+x^2)^2
    # which is negative for |x| < 1 and positive for |x| > 1.
    # So Cauchy is NOT log-concave. Good.
    err <- tryCatch({
      ars(dcauchy, n = 1000, lb = -100, ub = 100)
      FALSE
    }, error = function(e) grepl("[Ll]og-concav", e$message))
    err
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Standard Normal) ----
  cat("--- Standard Normal Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_normal_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 5000 points from N(0,1)...\n")
    set.seed(42)
    samples <- ars(n = 5000, f = dnorm, lb = -Inf, ub = Inf)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~0)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))

    # Save samples
    writeLines(as.character(samples), "/app/normal_samples.txt")
    cat("  Saved samples to /app/normal_samples.txt\n")

    # KS test against N(0,1)
    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pnorm")
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    # Check: mean close to 0, sd close to 1, KS p-value > 0.01
    abs(mean(samples)) < 0.15 && abs(sd(samples) - 1) < 0.15 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  run_test("test_normal_bounded", {
    cat("  Sampling 2000 points from N(0,1) on [-5, 5]...\n")
    set.seed(123)
    samples <- ars(dnorm, c(-5, 5), n = 2000)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), "\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), "\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Min: ", round(min(samples), 4), ", Max: ", round(max(samples), 4), "\n"))

    # All samples should be within bounds
    all(samples >= -5 & samples <= 5) && abs(mean(samples)) < 0.2
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Exponential) ----
  cat("--- Exponential Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_exponential_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 5000 points from Exp(1) on [0, 100]...\n")
    set.seed(99)
    samples <- ars(dexp, c(0.001, 100), n = 5000)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))

    # Save samples
    writeLines(as.character(samples), "/app/exponential_samples.txt")
    cat("  Saved samples to /app/exponential_samples.txt\n")

    # KS test
    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pexp")
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples) - 1) < 0.15 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Gamma) ----
  cat("--- Gamma Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_gamma_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 3000 points from Gamma(3, 1) on [0.01, 30]...\n")
    set.seed(77)
    gamma_dens <- function(x) dgamma(x, shape = 3, rate = 1)
    samples <- ars(gamma_dens, c(0.01, 30), n = 3000)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~3)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1.73)\n"))

    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pgamma", shape = 3, rate = 1)
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples) - 3) < 0.3 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Beta) ----
  cat("--- Beta Distribution Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_beta_sampling", {
    cat("  Sampling 3000 points from Beta(2, 5) on [0.01, 0.99]...\n")
    set.seed(55)
    beta_dens <- function(x) dbeta(x, shape1 = 2, shape2 = 5)
    samples <- ars(beta_dens, c(0.01, 0.99), n = 3000)
    expected_mean <- 2 / (2 + 5)
    expected_sd <- sqrt(2 * 5 / ((2 + 5)^2 * (2 + 5 + 1)))
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4),
               " (expected ~", round(expected_mean, 4), ")\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4),
               " (expected ~", round(expected_sd, 4), ")\n"))

    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pbeta", shape1 = 2, shape2 = 5)
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples) - expected_mean) < 0.05 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Sampling Tests (Log-Normal via unnormalized density) ----
  cat("--- Unnormalized Density Test ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_unnormalized_density", {
    cat("  Sampling from unnormalized Gaussian: f(x) = exp(-x^2/2)...\n")
    set.seed(33)
    unnorm_gauss <- function(x) exp(-x^2 / 2)
    samples <- ars(unnorm_gauss, n = 3000, lb = -Inf, ub = Inf)
    cat(paste0("  Sample mean: ", round(mean(samples), 4), " (expected ~0)\n"))
    cat(paste0("  Sample sd:   ", round(sd(samples), 4), " (expected ~1)\n"))

    ks <- ks.test(samples, "pnorm")
    cat(paste0("  KS test p-value: ", round(ks$p.value, 4), "\n"))

    abs(mean(samples)) < 0.15 && abs(sd(samples) - 1) < 0.15 && ks$p.value > 0.01
  })

  # ---- Module-Level Tests ----
  cat("--- Module-Level Tests ---\n\n")

  run_test("test_eval_log_density", {
    cat("  Testing eval_log_density with dnorm...\n")
    result <- eval_log_density(dnorm, 0)
    expected <- log(dnorm(0))
    cat(paste0("  log(dnorm(0)) = ", round(result, 6), " (expected ", round(expected, 6), ")\n"))
    abs(result - expected) < 1e-10
  })

  run_test("test_check_log_concavity_valid", {
    cat("  Testing log-concavity check on concave data...\n")
    # h(x) = -x^2 is concave
    x <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)
    h <- -x^2
    result <- tryCatch({ check_log_concavity(x, h); TRUE },
                       error = function(e) FALSE)
    result
  })

  run_test("test_check_log_concavity_invalid", {
    cat("  Testing log-concavity check on convex data...\n")
    # h(x) = x^2 is convex (NOT concave)
    x <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)
    h <- x^2
    result <- tryCatch({ check_log_concavity(x, h); FALSE },
                       error = function(e) grepl("[Ll]og-concav", e$message))
    result
  })

  run_test("test_upper_hull_above_density", {
    cat("  Testing that upper hull is above log-density at test points...\n")
    set.seed(11)
    x_abs <- c(-3, -1, 0, 1, 3)
    h_abs <- eval_log_density(dnorm, x_abs)
    hull <- compute_upper_hull(x_abs, h_abs, -Inf, Inf)

    # Check at many random points
    test_pts <- seq(-2.5, 2.5, by = 0.1)
    h_test <- eval_log_density(dnorm, test_pts)
    u_test <- eval_upper_hull(test_pts, hull)

    all_above <- all(u_test >= h_test - 1e-10)
    cat(paste0("  Upper hull >= log-density at all ", length(test_pts), " test points: ", all_above, "\n"))
    all_above
  })

  run_test("test_lower_hull_below_density", {
    cat("  Testing that lower hull is below log-density at test points...\n")
    x_abs <- c(-3, -1, 0, 1, 3)
    h_abs <- eval_log_density(dnorm, x_abs)

    test_pts <- seq(-2.5, 2.5, by = 0.1)
    h_test <- eval_log_density(dnorm, test_pts)
    l_test <- sapply(test_pts, function(x) eval_lower_hull(x, x_abs, h_abs))

    all_below <- all(l_test <= h_test + 1e-10)
    cat(paste0("  Lower hull <= log-density at all ", length(test_pts), " test points: ", all_below, "\n"))
    all_below
  })

  # ---- Summary ----
  cat("============================================================\n")
  cat(paste0("  TOTAL: ", pass_count + fail_count, " tests, ",
             pass_count, " passed, ", fail_count, " failed\n"))
  cat("============================================================\n")

  invisible(list(passed = pass_count, failed = fail_count))
}

# Run tests if this script is executed directly
if (sys.nframe() == 0) {
  test()
}

+# Derivative-free implementation using secant-based upper hull construction.

#' @param abscissae sorted vector of x values
#' @return TRUE if log-concave, else stops with error
-#' Compute the slopes between consecutive abscissae on the log-density
-#' The upper hull at each abscissa j uses the tangent lines from adjacent segments.
-#' For j=1: use slope from segment (x1, x2)
-#' For j=k (last): use slope from segment (x_{k-1}, x_k)
-#' For interior j: the tangent from left segment and right segment
+#' Compute the upper hull for the ARS algorithm (derivative-free version)
-#' Returns the intersection points z between consecutive tangent lines,
-#' plus the slopes and intercepts of each tangent segment.
+#' For k abscissae with log-density values, the upper hull is piecewise linear
+#' and lies above the log-density everywhere. It is constructed from the secant
+#' slopes between consecutive abscissae. By log-concavity, secant slopes
+#' At each interior abscissa x_j, the upper hull has a "tent" with:
+#'   - left arm:  slope s_j (right secant), valid for x <= x_j
+#'   - right arm: slope s_{j-1} (left secant), valid for x >= x_j
-#' @return list with z (intersection points), slopes, intercepts for upper hull
+#' @return list with segment info for the piecewise linear upper hull
+  if (k < 3) stop("Need at least 3 abscissae for upper hull construction.")
-  # Slopes of secant lines between consecutive abscissae
+  # Secant slopes: s[j] = slope from x_j to x_{j+1}
-  # Each tangent line j: u_j(x) = h_j + s_j * (x - x_j)
-  # where s_j is the slope assigned to abscissa j
-  # For the upper hull in ARS:
-  #   - segment j uses the line through (x_j, h_j) and (x_{j+1}, h_{j+1})
-  #   i.e., slope = secant_slope[j], intercept at x_j
-  # There are k-1 segments. The hull extends to lb on the left and ub on the right.
+  # Build the list of line segments for the upper hull.
+  # Each segment is defined by a point (px, ph) and slope m:
+  #   u(x) = ph + m * (x - px) = (ph - m*px) + m*x
+  # 1. x_1's left arm: slope s[1], through (x_1, h_1), for x from lb to z_1
+  # Then for each interior point j = 2,...,k-1:
+  #   left arm:  slope s[j], through (x_j, h_j)
+  #   right arm: slope s[j-1], through (x_j, h_j)
+  # Last: x_k's right arm: slope s[k-1], through (x_k, h_k), for x from z_{k-1} to ub
-  # The upper hull consists of k-1 line segments connecting consecutive points.
-  # But in ARS, the upper hull is defined by tangent lines at each abscissa.
-  # For log-concave densities, the tangent at x_j has slope between
-  # the left secant and right secant.
-  # Actually, in Gilks & Wild (1992), the upper hull u_k(x) is the
-  # minimum of the tangent lines. With only function values (no derivatives),
-  # they use the secant lines. Specifically:
-  # The upper hull is piecewise linear with pieces defined by:
-  #   piece j (for j = 1, ..., k-1): line through (x_j, h_j) and (x_{j+1}, h_{j+1})
-  # The intersection z_j of piece j and piece j+1 gives the breakpoints.
+  seg_px[idx] <- abscissae[1]; seg_ph[idx] <- h_vals[1]; seg_m[idx] <- s[1]
-  # Intersection of line j and line j+1:
-  #   h_j + slope_j * (z - x_j) = h_{j+1} + slope_{j+1} * (z - x_{j+1})
-  #   z = (h_{j+1} - h_j + slope_j * x_j - slope_{j+1} * x_{j+1}) / (slope_j - slope_{j+1})
+    seg_px[idx] <- abscissae[j]; seg_ph[idx] <- h_vals[j]; seg_m[idx] <- s[j]
+    seg_px[idx] <- abscissae[j]; seg_ph[idx] <- h_vals[j]; seg_m[idx] <- s[j-1]
-  if (k < 2) stop("Need at least 2 abscissae for upper hull.")
+  seg_px[idx] <- abscissae[k]; seg_ph[idx] <- h_vals[k]; seg_m[idx] <- s[k-1]
+  # Convert to intercept form: u(x) = intercept + slope * x
-  # Intersection points z between consecutive segments
-  # z[j] is where segment j meets segment j+1 (for j = 1,...,k-2)
+      # Same point (left/right arm of same tent): breakpoint is at that x
-  # Full z vector including domain bounds
#' Evaluate the upper hull at a point x
+#' @param x scalar or vector of points
-  # Find which segment x falls in
#' Evaluate the lower hull (piecewise linear through abscissae) at a point x
#' @return l(x), the lower hull value. -Inf if outside the range of abscissae.
-  # Find which interval x falls in
#' Sample from the upper hull (piecewise-exponential distribution)
-#' The upper hull is a piecewise linear function on log scale, which corresponds
#' @param n number of samples to draw

-  #   if b_j != 0: exp(a_j) * (exp(b_j * z_{j+1}) - exp(b_j * z_j)) / b_j
+  # Compute area under each segment: integral of exp(b + m*x) from z[j] to z[j+1]
-      # Integral of exp(a + b*x) from lo to hi
+    # Handle infinite bounds: for the integral to converge, slope must be favorable
+      # Integral of exp(b + m*x) from -Inf to hi = exp(b + m*hi) / m
+      # Integral of exp(b + m*x) from lo to Inf = -exp(b + m*lo) / m
+      # For numerical stability, factor out the larger value
+      areas[j] <- exp(log_max) * (exp(log_hi - log_max) - exp(log_lo - log_max)) / m[j]
-    stop("Upper hull has zero or infinite area. Check domain bounds.")
